Jahmyr Gibbs.summary: Jahmyr Gibbs is coming off his best season yet, totaling 1,839 scrimmage yards and 18 touchdowns across 17 games in 2025 — good for RB3 in half-PPR. With David Montgomery traded to Houston, he enters 2026 as Detroit's unquestioned lead back, poised to absorb the additional touches as a true three-down workhorse. He's a three-time Pro Bowler before his age-25 season and one of the most complete backs in the game. Gibbs should be taken either #1 or #2 overall in half-PPR formats.

Bijan Robinson.summary: Bijan Robinson earned first-team All-Pro honors in 2025 after leading the NFL with 2,298 scrimmage yards, the most in Falcons history, on 287 carries and 79 receptions. He enters 2026 with an even cleaner path to the end zone after Tyler Allgeier, who scored eight rushing TDs last season, departed in free agency. Despite quarterback uncertainty in Atlanta, Robinson has produced at an elite level in every season of his career. With a cleaner path to the end zone than ever before and elite production in every season of his career, Robinson is one of the most compelling early-round investments in 2026.

Ja'Marr Chase.summary: Ja'Marr Chase put up 125 catches for 1,412 yards in 2025 on a team that finished 6-11, showcasing his ability to produce regardless of what's happening around him. He enters 2026 as the near consensus WR1 in fantasy drafts, with Joe Burrow healthy and Tee Higgins returning as a legitimate complementary threat. At just 26 years old, his peak may still be ahead of him. With Burrow healthy and a full supporting cast around him, Chase is the rare receiver with both a sky-high floor and legitimate WR1 upside from start to finish.

Puka Nacua.summary: Puka Nacua led the NFL in receptions in 2025 with 129 catches for 1,715 yards and 10 touchdowns, earning the top PFF receiving grade among all wide receivers. He returns in 2026 as the clear WR1 in Los Angeles with Matthew Stafford healthy and an offense built around getting him the ball. Entering just his third NFL season and having yet to show a true ceiling, Nacua is one of the most exciting early-round receivers in 2026 fantasy drafts.

Jaxon Smith-Njigba.summary: Jaxon Smith-Njigba won the 2025 NFL Offensive Player of the Year award after leading the league with 1,793 receiving yards on 119 catches and 10 touchdowns, becoming the highest-paid wide receiver in league history on a new extension. He commanded a 32.6% target share (the highest in the NFL) in Seattle's run-heavy offense and carried the Seahawks to a Super Bowl. As the reigning Offensive Player of the Year and the highest-paid receiver in NFL history, JSN enters 2026 as one of the most locked-in WR1 options on the board.

Christian McCaffrey.summary: Christian McCaffrey posted 2,126 scrimmage yards and 17 touchdowns in 2025, finishing as the RB1 despite averaging just 3.9 yards per carry, a testament to his elite receiving production of 102 catches for 924 yards. Entering his age-30 season, durability remains the question after he led the NFL in touches and missed 13 games in 2024. When healthy, McCaffrey is the most complete back in football — but his injury history makes him a boom-or-bust early-round pick that requires a handcuff strategy.

Jonathan Taylor.summary: Jonathan Taylor led the NFL in both rushing attempts (323) and rushing touchdowns (18) in 2025, totaling 1,585 rushing yards with 46 receptions for 378 yards. He enters 2026 as the unquestioned bell cow in Indianapolis, with a healthy Daniel Jones expected back under center. As the NFL's leading rusher and touchdown scorer in 2025, Taylor enters 2026 as one of the most reliable workhorse backs available in the early rounds.

De'Von Achane.summary: De'Von Achane recorded a career-high 1,838 scrimmage yards and 12 touchdowns in 2025 while leading the NFL with a 5.7 yards per carry average (all while serving as the centerpiece of a Dolphins offense that lost Tyreek Hill in Week 4). He signed a four-year, $68M extension this offseason and is locked in as Miami's featured back heading into a rebuild year with Malik Willis at quarterback. Achane's elite efficiency makes him a compelling early-round target even in a rebuilding offensive environment.

Amon-Ra St. Brown.summary: Amon-Ra St. Brown has posted 100+ receptions, 1,000+ yards, and double-digit touchdowns in each of the past four seasons, a run matched only by Marvin Harrison, Antonio Brown, Davante Adams, and Stefon Diggs. His 2025 line of 117 catches for 1,401 yards and 11 TDs earned him his fourth straight Pro Bowl. St. Brown is one of the most reliable WR1 options available at his price in 2026 drafts and a guy with an extremely safe floor, having finished WR3 for 3 straight seasons.

CeeDee Lamb.summary: CeeDee Lamb posted 1,077 yards and three touchdowns across 14 games in 2025 — a down year by his standards, partly due to injury and partly due to George Pickens' emergence as a co-star. It was still his fifth straight 1,000-yard season, and he out-targeted Pickens by 2.6 per game in games both played. With Prescott healthy and Dallas returning one of the NFL's top passing offenses, Lamb is a strong bounce-back candidate with WR1 upside in 2026.

Ashton Jeanty.summary: Ashton Jeanty's rookie season was held back by a Raiders offensive line that ranked among the worst in the league: he averaged just 3.3 yards per carry on inside runs, and the team ranked 31st in red zone scoring attempts. The talent was never in question (he ranked first in opportunity share among qualified backs), and Las Vegas enters 2026 with a significantly upgraded line (Tyler Linderbaum, Kolton Miller returning) and new head coach Klint Kubiak. With better blocking and a cleaner situation around him, Jeanty is one of the most compelling bounce-back candidates in early drafts.

Justin Jefferson.summary: Justin Jefferson's 2025 season was derailed by some of the worst quarterback play in the league (J.J. McCarthy, Max Brosmer), finishing with just two touchdowns and a WR31 PPG finish — a staggering fall from his usual top-10 production. The talent has never been in doubt; Jefferson has been one of the most physically gifted receivers of his generation. Kyler Murray arrives in Minnesota in 2026, and if he can provide even league-average QB play, Jefferson's bounce-back potential at current ADP is significant.

James Cook III.summary: James Cook led the NFL in rushing yards in 2025 (1,621) and finished with 1,912 scrimmage yards and 14 touchdowns on 342 touches across 17 games. He's a three-time Pro Bowler who has improved his fantasy output every season of his career, locked in on a four-year, $48M extension. New head coach Joe Brady was previously Buffalo's offensive coordinator, meaning little should change in how the Bills deploy their bell cow, making Cook one of the safest early-round picks in 2026.

A.J. Brown.summary: A.J. Brown finished 2025 with 78 receptions for 1,003 yards and 7 touchdowns in Philadelphia before being traded to New England this offseason, a significant situation change. Brown joins Drake Maye's Patriots as the clear WR1, inheriting a massive target share in an offense that projects for more passing volume than what the Eagles ran. He'll be 29 in 2026 with some injury history, but Brown has never finished below WR13 in a full healthy season, and the upgrade in opportunity could more than offset any age-related concerns.

Omarion Hampton.summary: Omarion Hampton's rookie season was cut short by injuries to both himself and the Chargers' offensive line (losing Rashawn Slater and Joe Alt), but in the two games he was fully healthy and protected, he totaled 294 scrimmage yards and two touchdowns. Both linemen are expected back in 2026, and the Chargers added Mike McDaniel as offensive coordinator — the same coach who turned Raheem Mostert and De'Von Achane into fantasy stars in Miami. Hampton enters 2026 as the clear bell cow in one of the most intriguing offensive situations in the league.

Kenneth Walker III.summary: Kenneth Walker III won Super Bowl MVP with Seattle before signing a three-year, $43M deal with Kansas City in free agency. His 2025 numbers (1,309 scrimmage yards, 5 TDs) were suppressed by a Charbonnet timeshare and red zone freeze-out, but he ranked second in explosive run rate and first in missed tackle rate among qualified backs. In Andy Reid's offense (which has featured a 40+ catch RB in two of the last five seasons), Walker has legitimate RB1 upside — with the only real caveat being Patrick Mahomes' recovery from an ACL tear.

Brock Bowers.summary: Brock Bowers set the NFL record for most receptions by a rookie in league history in 2024 (112), then had his 2025 season limited to 12 games by a knee injury (64 receptions, 680 yards, 7 TDs). He enters 2026 fully healthy with a new offensive ecosystem around him under head coach Klint Kubiak, and a quarterback room that could include Fernando Mendoza or Kirk Cousins. At just 23 years old, Bowers is the most talented tight end prospect in a long time, and the talent gap between him and the TE3+ tier makes him one of the highest-upside picks at his position.

George Pickens.summary: George Pickens broke out in his first season in Dallas in 2025 (93 receptions, 1,429 yards, 9 TDs), earning Pro Bowl honors and establishing himself as one of the most dangerous contested-catch receivers in football. He enters 2026 on the franchise tag with Dallas, returning to Dak Prescott and an offense that finished second in the NFL in passing. At 25 years old and on a prove-it year (with long-term contract talks still ongoing), Pickens has every incentive to build on a breakout that most of the league didn't see coming.

Chase Brown.summary: Chase Brown totaled 1,456 scrimmage yards and 11 touchdowns in 2025 — and Burrow missed 9 games. When Burrow returned in Week 13, Brown averaged 22.3 PPR points per game over the final six weeks of the season (RB2 over that stretch). He enters 2026 as the unquestioned lead back in Cincinnati on a contract year, with everything to prove alongside a fully healthy Burrow. The opportunity and situation are both pointing in the same direction.

Saquon Barkley.summary: Saquon Barkley had a significant step back in 2025 (1,140 rushing yards, 7 TDs) following his historic 2024 campaign, and the narrative around him is shakier than it's been in years, especially with A.J. Brown now gone and Philadelphia's offense in flux. That said, Barkley is still just 29, still has elite burst and pass-catching ability, and remains the focal point of the Eagles' backfield with no real competition. The floor is lower than it was a year ago, but the upside in a full-health season in Philly remains genuinely appealing at the right price.

Josh Allen.summary: Josh Allen finished as the QB1 in fantasy for the fourth time in the last six seasons in 2025, totaling 3,668 passing yards, 25 passing touchdowns, 579 rushing yards, and 14 rushing touchdowns — 39 total TDs in 17 games. The dual-threat floor is unmatched at the position; he's the only quarterback to rush for 500+ yards in each of the last five seasons. He enters 2026 with a new weapon in DJ Moore and a foot surgery behind him, making him the consensus QB1 for a reason — the floor alone justifies early-round QB consideration in 2026 drafts.

Drake London.summary: Drake London's 2025 season was limited to 12 games by a shoulder injury and a knee injury (68 catches, 919 yards, 7 TDs), but in games where he was fully healthy, he averaged 11.2 targets and 24.2 PPR points (numbers that rival any receiver in football). He just signed a four-year, $141M extension in Atlanta, signaling the franchise's commitment to him as their WR1 for years to come. At 24 years old entering his fifth season, a full healthy year from London is one of the more compelling bounce-back plays in the entire draft.

Derrick Henry.summary: Derrick Henry posted 1,595 rushing yards and 16 touchdowns on 307 carries in 2025 (5.2 YPC), and is just the 32nd player in NFL history to surpass 10,000 career rushing yards. Everyone keeps waiting for the cliff — it hasn't come. He re-signed with Baltimore on a two-year extension in 2025 and returns to a Ravens offense that led the league in goal-line carries, keeping his TD upside as high as any back in football. The age concern is real at 32, but so is the production.

Trey McBride.summary: Trey McBride set the all-time NFL record for receptions by a tight end in a single season in 2025 (126), finishing with 1,239 yards and 11 touchdowns, earning first-team All-Pro honors and becoming the first TE in league history with 100+ receptions in back-to-back seasons. He signed a four-year, $76M extension in 2025 and is locked in as Arizona's offensive focal point. The gap between McBride and the TE3+ tier in fantasy is as large as it's been at any position in recent memory, making him a target in the early rounds for positional dominance over your league-mates.

Garrett Wilson.summary: Garrett Wilson's 2025 season was cut short by a knee injury that cost him 10 games — but in the six weeks before going down, he was averaging 9.3 targets per game and pacing for a 100-catch, 11-TD season. He enters 2026 fully healthy with Geno Smith now at quarterback, coming over from Las Vegas. Wilson was the WR7 in 2024 with a middling QB situation; with Smith under center, the ceiling is much higher than it was with Justin Fields, and the current ADP represents a significant discount on one of the most gifted route runners in the game.

Rashee Rice.summary: Rashee Rice missed most of 2025 with a knee injury (torn ACL suffered in 2024) but is now trending healthy heading into 2026. Before going down in Week 4 of the 2024 season, he was the WR8 in PPR points per game. He returns to Patrick Mahomes — still one of the best quarterbacks in football even coming back from his own knee injury — as the clear WR1 in Kansas City with Kenneth Walker now the focal point of the run game. If both are healthy, Rice is one of the better value plays in the second round. Obviously, the offseason probation violation and ongoing off-the-field issues are worth monitoring for Rice who still has some question marks attached to his name ahead of draft season.

Nico Collins.summary: Nico Collins continued to be a solid WR1 in fantasy in 2025, finishing with 1,117 receiving yards and nine touchdowns as C.J. Stroud's go-to target. He ranked top-10 among all receivers in yards per route run and has back-to-back 1,000-yard seasons now on his resume. At 27 entering his prime, Collins has solidified himself as a reliable WR1 option in a Texans offense with genuine Super Bowl aspirations. The WR room around him is thin enough that his target share should remain intact in 2026. Stroud's emergence as one of the better QBs in the NFL is the real question mark with Collins.

Breece Hall.summary: Breece Hall had a frustrating 2025 season (1,100 scrimmage yards, 6 TDs) as the Jets cycled through bad quarterback play and a run-heavy scheme that didn't complement his receiving skills. He enters 2026 with Geno Smith now under center, and the Jets added some solid offensive skill position players in the NFL draft to complement their attack. Hall's receiving ceiling has always been the thing that separates him from a standard RB2, and with Geno a more legitimate threat to feed him with targets, that ceiling is finally worth buying into.

Travis Etienne Jr..summary: Travis Etienne Jr. is entering a new chapter in New Orleans after departing Jacksonville, signing with the Saints this offseason. He posted 1,341 scrimmage yards and 11 touchdowns in 2025 — his third straight 1,000-yard season — and arrives in an offense with Tyler Shough at quarterback and a clean backfield to himself. The Saints have typically leaned on their backs historically, and Etienne (27) has shown he can handle a full workload. New situation worth monitoring as training camp approaches, and Alvin Kamara is aging out but is still in town, though the talent and track record with Etienne Jr. are not in question.

Malik Nabers.summary: Malik Nabers' 2026 situation is defined almost entirely by his recovery from ACL surgery. When healthy in 2025, he was producing at a WR1 level before going down, and that was when Russell Wilson was still the starting QB for the G-Men. He enters 2026 pairing with Jaxson Dart, one of the most explosive young passers in football and a guy we haven't seen him build chemistry with yet. The upside here is as high as any receiver in the draft, but the injury timeline means he could miss the early weeks of the season. The reward is considerable for those willing to take the risk at current ADP.

Tetairoa McMillan.summary: Tetairoa McMillan won the AP Offensive Rookie of the Year award in 2025 after setting the Panthers' rookie record with 1,014 receiving yards (70 catches, 7 TDs) — doing it against inconsistent quarterback play on one of the league's lower-volume passing offenses. He enters 2026 as the unquestioned WR1 in Carolina with ongoing foot soreness to monitor, but the talent is clear and the opportunity to dominate targets in Year 2 is substantial. At just 23 years old with the physical tools to be a top receiver in this league for years, McMillan is a high-upside mid-round target.

Josh Jacobs.summary: Josh Jacobs posted 1,211 scrimmage yards and 14 touchdowns across 15 games in 2025, his second straight season as one of the NFL's leading touchdown scorers in Green Bay. The concern is age (entering his age-28 season) and a rash of late-season calf, knee, and ankle issues — with his PPG dipping significantly after Week 10. When healthy, Jacobs is a rock-solid RB2 with strong floor in a Packers offense that supports his workload. It's also worth monitoring Josh Jacobs' arrest linked to domestic violence allegations heading into draft season, as there's significant uncertainty lingering beyond his on-field potential.

Kyren Williams.summary: Kyren Williams quietly had a strong 2025 season as Sean McVay's featured back (780 rushing yards, 8 TDs on 152 carries), operating in somewhat of a timeshare with Blake Corum but commanding the passing-down and red zone work throughout. He enters 2026 as the incumbent lead back in one of the best offenses in football, with Matthew Stafford (MVP) returning and Puka Nacua and Davante Adams keeping defenses honest. The only risk is the Corum committee and his own injury history (missed significant time in 2024). When healthy, Williams has RB1 upside in this offense.

Colston Loveland.summary: Colston Loveland had a breakout finish to his rookie season in 2025 (58 receptions, 713 yards, 6 TDs), earning TE2 fantasy honors from Week 9 onward — behind only Trey McBride. Over his final four regular-season games and two playoff appearances, he averaged 12 targets per game, culminating in an 8-catch, 137-yard performance in the playoff win over Green Bay. With Ben Johnson developing a clear chemistry preference for Loveland alongside Caleb Williams, he profiles as a top-3 TE in 2026 fantasy drafts with significant upside still ahead.

Terry McLaurin.summary: Terry McLaurin's 2025 season was derailed by a quad injury that limited him to 10 games (38 receptions, 582 yards, 3 TDs) — a sharp drop from his All-Pro 2024 campaign (13 TDs, 1,000+ yards). Even while banged up, he ranked 9th among all wide receivers in PFF receiving grade and posted an impressive 15.3 yards per reception. He enters 2026 at age 31 as Washington's clear WR1 with Jayden Daniels, a dual-threat playmaker who lit up the league in 2024, returning from his own injuries. A full healthy season from both is a compelling bounce-back setup.

Javonte Williams.summary: Javonte Williams had a career year in 2025 (1,201 rushing yards, 11 TDs, 4.8 YPC), establishing himself as one of the most efficient backs in the NFC after some frustrating years with the Broncos and almost no off-season hype. He re-signed with Dallas on a three-year deal and returns to one of the NFL's top passing offenses (2nd in the NFL in 2025), where defenses face a constant threat from CeeDee Lamb and George Pickens keeping boxes light. With no real competition in the backfield and a proven ceiling, Williams is a reliable mid-round target with RB1 upside.

Chris Olave.summary: Chris Olave broke out in 2025 with career highs in catches (100), targets (156), receiving yards (1,163), and touchdowns (9) — building excellent chemistry with rookie quarterback Tyler Shough in Kellen Moore's offense. He enters 2026 as the likely WR1 in New Orleans in Year 2 of Moore's system, with Shough's expected development as his primary upside lever. At 26 years old entering his prime and coming off his cleanest full season yet, Olave is one of the more quietly reliable WR2 picks on the board. The concussion history is the main note worth consideration, as is Jordyn Tyson being added to this offense and Travis Etienne's ability as a pass-catcher.

Emeka Egbuka.summary: Emeka Egbuka's rookie season (63 receptions, 938 yards, 6 TDs) was defined by a blistering start (WR3 in PPR Weeks 1-5) and a quiet finish after a hamstring injury robbed him of his top speed. With Mike Evans now in San Francisco, Egbuka enters 2026 as Tampa Bay's unquestioned WR1 with a dedicated role outside and a clear path to 130+ targets. At 23 years old with elite tools and a healthy offseason behind him, the upside for a true WR1 breakout in Year 2 is very real.

Jeremiyah Love.summary: Jeremiyah Love is the most electrifying running back prospect since Bijan Robinson — a Doak Walker Award winner who posted 3,014 yards and 40 TDs over his final two seasons at Notre Dame and ran a 4.36 40-yard dash at 212 pounds. Drafted 3rd overall by Arizona, the fantasy concern is a crowded backfield (Tyler Allgeier, James Conner) on a team projected among the league's worst. The talent is undeniable but the situation warrants patience — Love is a dynasty cornerstone best targeted as a high-upside RB2 in redraft until the backfield picture clarifies.

DeVonta Smith.summary: DeVonta Smith finished 2025 with 77 receptions for 1,008 yards and 4 touchdowns — a serviceable but unspectacular line for a player with WR1 talent. With A.J. Brown now traded to New England, Smith enters 2026 as Philadelphia's clear WR1 with a dramatically expanded target share up for grabs. He showed his ceiling in a Week 7 performance against the Vikings (9 catches, 183 yards, 1 TD) and has quietly been one of the most efficient receivers in the NFL over his career. The situation upgrade is significant, giving Smith one of the highest ceilings in drafts this year.

Zay Flowers.summary: Zay Flowers had his best NFL season in 2025, posting career highs in receptions (86), yards (1,211), and a WR12 PPG finish — ranking 8th in target share (27.7%) and 7th in yards per route run. The only area lagging behind is touchdown production (just 5 in 2025), as his red zone usage has been limited relative to his overall role. He enters 2026 with Lamar Jackson healthy and a new offensive coordinator in Declan Doyle tasked with getting more out of the position near the goal line. If the TD rate normalizes, Flowers has legitimate WR1 upside in what remains one of the league's elite offenses.

Tee Higgins.summary: Tee Higgins posted a career-high 11 touchdowns in 2025 (59 receptions, 846 yards) despite playing just 15 games and catching passes from backup quarterbacks for a significant stretch while Burrow recovered from a toe injury. In games with Burrow healthy, Higgins averaged 13.3 half-PPR points per game — WR9-caliber production. He enters 2026 at 27 years old with Burrow fully healthy, no contract disputes, and the same elite supporting cast. The durability question is real (he's missed 12 games over three seasons), but the upside when available is among the highest at the position.

Lamar Jackson.summary: Lamar Jackson's 2025 season was derailed by hamstring and back injuries that limited him to 13 games (2,549 passing yards, 21 TDs, 349 rushing yards). Even in a compromised year, his per-game rushing production remained elite — the concern is simply staying healthy. With a new head coach (Jesse Minter) and OC (Declan Doyle) in Baltimore, plus Zay Flowers healthy and Derrick Henry returning, the offensive pieces are in place. When healthy, Jackson has finished as a top-2 fantasy QB in four of the last six seasons, and the bounce-back case for 2026 is straightforward.

D'Andre Swift.summary: D'Andre Swift posted career highs in rushing yards (1,087) and touchdowns (9) in 2025 under Ben Johnson, finishing as the RB15 in PPR — his best fantasy season to date at 4.9 yards per carry. The concern entering 2026 is a growing timeshare with sophomore Kyle Monangai, who ate into Swift's snap share as the season progressed. Swift enters his contract year with something to prove, but the backfield competition is real and worth monitoring heading into training camp. Still, Swift has finished as an RB2 for 6 straight seasons and represents a pretty safe floor at the position.

Tyler Warren.summary: Tyler Warren had an impressive rookie season in 2025 (76 receptions, 817 yards, 4 TDs), finishing as the TE5 in fantasy points per game through his first 10 games before a late-season dip coincided with Daniel Jones' health issues. With the Colts trading Michael Pittman this offseason, Warren becomes Indianapolis' clear No. 1 pass-catcher — a massive opportunity for a 23-year-old tight end entering Year 2. His target share could jump significantly in 2026 with very little receiver competition ahead of him.

Davante Adams.summary: Davante Adams led the NFL in touchdown catches in 2025 with 14 (on 60 receptions for 789 yards) — his third time leading the league in TD receptions across three different teams. He was the TD-dependent WR2 behind Puka Nacua in a historically efficient Rams offense, and he enters 2026 on the second year of his deal with Matthew Stafford returning. At 33 years old with a hamstring issue that cost him three regular season games, the floor is lower than it's ever been — but the touchdown ceiling in this offense remains as real as ever.

Carnell Tate.summary: Carnell Tate was selected 4th overall in the 2026 NFL Draft by the Tennessee Titans, the highest a wide receiver has gone in years. The Ohio State product averaged 3.17 yards per route run in college with zero drops in 2025 and brings elite route-running polish to a Titans offense built around Cam Ward. He enters a receiver room without a clear established No. 1, making him the presumptive WR1 from Day 1. The upside of a top-4 pick in a wide-open offense could make Tate one of the more compelling WR2 dart throws in deeper redraft leagues.

Cam Skattebo.summary: Cam Skattebo was on a historic pace as a rookie before a compound ankle dislocation and fibula fracture ended his season in Week 8 — he averaged 16.1 fantasy points per game (RB9) across his six starts, with a 15% target share and dominance at the goal line. He's cleared for 11-on-11 drills at mandatory minicamp and remains atop New York's depth chart with no significant competition added. If he's fully healthy for Week 1, Skattebo has genuine RB1 upside in an offense that leans heavily on its running back.

Ladd McConkey.summary: Ladd McConkey had a step back in his second NFL season (66 receptions, 789 yards, 6 TDs in 16 games) after a breakout 2024 rookie year, and currently has a hamstring strain that kept him out of team drills at OTAs. The talent and role are both unquestioned — he's the clear WR1 in LA with Justin Herbert healthy and a Harbaugh offense that runs the ball enough to keep defenses honest. A return to his rookie-year efficiency in a full healthy season gives him legitimate WR2 upside at his current ADP. Not to mention, Mike McDaniel could use McConkey in a lot of different ways that could turn him into 2026's version of JSN.

Quinshon Judkins.summary: Quinshon Judkins showed exactly why he was so coveted as a prospect in his rookie season — 867 rushing yards and 7 TDs on 230 carries before a dislocated ankle and fractured fibula ended his year in December. He's progressed to team drills at OTAs and appears on track for training camp. With a revamped offensive line and Todd Monken's new offense in Cleveland, Judkins enters 2026 as one of the most compelling Year 2 breakout candidates in the draft — as long as the fibula recovery holds.
